1: A theme: The Bethel delegation's question about the fasting practices — seeking YHWH's will about religious observance — is the background for Christ's teaching on the spirit vs. the letter of religious practice. 'These people honor me with their lips, but their heart is far from me; in vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men' (Matthew 15:8-9, citing Isaiah 29:13). The question about fasting ritual opens the deeper question about the heart of covenant religion that Christ addresses throughout the Sermon on the Mount.
2: A theme: The delegation comes 'to entreat the favor of the LORD' — seeking YHWH's face. Christ is the face of YHWH: 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9). To seek the favor of YHWH is now to come to Christ: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28). The Bethel delegation seeking YHWH's favor anticipates every person who comes seeking the face of God and finds it in the face of Jesus Christ.
3: A theme: The question 'Should I weep and abstain in the fifth month?' is the religious person's sincere question about how to honor God. Christ addresses the same question about fasting: 'When you fast, do not look gloomy like the hypocrites' (Matthew 6:16). The proper motive for fasting — not for human approval or ritual obligation but for genuine relationship with YHWH — is the same criterion Christ applies. The Bethel delegation's sincere question receives YHWH's answer through Zechariah; the sincere question about how to honor God receives Christ's answer in the Sermon on the Mount.
4: A theme: 'The word of the LORD of hosts came to me' — the divine response to a human question about religious practice. Christ is the living Word who responds to every human question about how to relate to God: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6). The divine word that redirects the Bethel question from ritual to heart is Christ himself, who embodies the answer to the deepest human question: how does one stand before God?
5: A theme: 'Was it for me that you fasted?' — the penetrating divine question about religious motivation. Christ applies the same criterion: 'Beware of practicing your righteousness before other people in order to be seen by them, for then you will have no reward from your Father who is in heaven' (Matthew 6:1). The Beatitudes' 'blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God' (Matthew 5:8) is the positive form of YHWH's negative question: fasting, praying, and giving done from a pure heart — not for human approval — is done 'for YHWH.'
6: A theme: 'When you eat and drink, do you not eat and drink for yourselves?' The self-referential nature of religious practice disconnected from God is what Christ criticizes in the Pharisees and what Paul addresses: 'So, whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God' (1 Corinthians 10:31). The transformation from self-referential religion to God-referential religion is the new covenant's gift: Christ's Spirit enables the doing-all-to-God's-glory that the unaided will cannot sustain.
7: A theme: YHWH's reference to the 'former prophets' is Christ's reference to the Scriptures. 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matthew 5:17). Christ is the fulfillment of all the former prophets urged: justice, kindness, humility before YHWH (Micah 6:8). He does not deliver a new message but embodies the old message in its fullness.
8: A theme: The formal oracle introduction preceding the ethical summary (vv9-10) models the divine authority behind Christ's ethical teaching. 'You have heard that it was said... but I say to you' (Matthew 5:21-48) — Christ speaks with the same 'Thus says the LORD' authority that the prophets wielded, but directly, in his own name, as the final authority. The 'word of the LORD came to me' in Zechariah becomes 'I say to you' in Christ.
9: A direct revelation: 'Render true judgments, show kindness and mercy to one another.' The three ethical imperatives — justice, kindness (chesed), mercy (rachamim) — are the OT's social-covenant ethics that Christ embodies and teaches. 'Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you tithe mint and dill and cumin, and have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness' (Matthew 23:23). The three terms of Zechariah's ethical oracle correspond exactly to the three 'weightier matters' Christ names. Christ is himself the embodiment of all three: the just judge, the covenant-faithful lord, the compassionate healer.
10: A direct revelation: 'Do not oppress the widow, the fatherless, the sojourner, or the poor.' The four protected classes of covenant society — the most vulnerable — are the special objects of Christ's ministry. 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor' (Luke 4:18). The blind receive their sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news proclaimed (Matthew 11:5). Christ's ministry is the direct fulfillment of the covenant obligation Zechariah articulates: the protection and blessing of the vulnerable.
11: A type: The stubborn shoulder and stopped ears of the fathers who refused the former prophets is the type of the rejection Christ experienced. 'He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11). 'Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often would I have gathered your children together... and you were not willing!' (Matthew 23:37). The fathers' stubborn rejection of the prophets reaches its climax in the generation's rejection of the prophets' fulfillment — the Son himself.
12: A type: The diamond-hard heart (lev shamir) that repelled the Torah and the words YHWH sent through the former prophets is the hardness of heart Christ addresses. 'He grieved at their hardness of heart' (Mark 3:5). The new covenant promise: 'I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh' (Ezekiel 36:26). The shamir-hard heart that rejected the word through the prophets is replaced, in Christ, by the heart of flesh that receives the Word himself.
13: A type: 'As I called, and they would not hear, so they called, and I would not hear.' The principle of reciprocal deafness — those who refused to hear YHWH's call will find YHWH not hearing their call in distress — is applied to the generation that rejected Christ. 'You will seek me and you will not find me. Where I am you cannot come' (John 7:34). The Jewish leaders' cry at the cross — 'His blood be on us and on our children!' (Matthew 27:25) — and the subsequent 70 CE judgment (Jerusalem's destruction) is the historical fulfillment of the reciprocal-deafness principle.
14: A type: YHWH scattering the hardened generation 'among all the nations with a storm-wind' and making the pleasant land desolate is fulfilled in the 70 CE destruction of Jerusalem and the diaspora that followed. Christ prophesied it directly: 'There will be great distress upon the earth and wrath against this people. They will fall by the edge of the sword and be led captive among all nations, and Jerusalem will be trampled underfoot by the Gentiles, until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled' (Luke 21:23-24). The desolation of Zechariah 7:14 is the template for the 70 CE desolation — the divine judgment on the generation that rejected the Son, as the earlier generation rejected the prophets.
